coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as sensitive fluorescence probes for selective recognition of cinnamaldehyde and l-Arginine/l-Lysine in living cells

coredata.dc:description: The disorder of amino acid metabolism and the abuse of small molecule drugs pose serious threats to public health. However, due to the limitations of existing detection technologies in sensing cinnamaldehyde (CAL) and l-Arginine/l-Lysine (l-Arg/l-Lys), there is an urgent need to develop new sensing strategies to meet the severe challenges currently facing. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were developed using a simple one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. These N-CDs exhibited numerous distinctive characteristics such as excellent photoluminescence, high water dispersibility, favorable biocompatibility, and superior chemical inertness. Strikingly, the as-prepared CDs as a highly efficient fluorescent probe possessed significant sensitivity and selectivity toward CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys over other analytes with a low detection limit of 58 nM and 16 nM/18 nM, respectively. The fluorescence of N-CDs could be quenched by CAL through an electron transfer process. Then, the strong electrostatic interaction between l-Arg/l-Lys and N-CDs induced the efficient fluorescence recovery. More importantly, the outstanding biosafety and excellent analyte-responsive fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs have also been verified in living cells as well as in serum and urine. Overall, the N-CDs had a wide application prospect in the diagnosis of amino acid metabolic diseases and small molecule drug sensing.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots as sensitive fluorescence probes for selective recognition of cinnamaldehyde and l-Arginine/l-Lysine in living cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One-pot synthesis of N-CDs was applied as a highly efficient “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent probe for the rapid detection of CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys in living cells. As a consequence, these N-CDs as a powerful analysis tool displayed more prominent and influential ability in real-time monitoring CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys, and might offer a new opportunity for exploring the physiological and pathological processes of amino acid metabolism diseases.

The disorder of amino acid metabolism and the abuse of small molecule drugs pose serious threats to public health. However, due to the limitations of existing detection technologies in sensing cinnamaldehyde (CAL) and l-Arginine/l-Lysine (l-Arg/l-Lys), there is an urgent need to develop new sensing strategies to meet the severe challenges currently facing. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were developed using a simple one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. These N-CDs exhibited numerous distinctive characteristics such as excellent photoluminescence, high water dispersibility, favorable biocompatibility, and superior chemical inertness. Strikingly, the as-prepared CDs as a highly efficient fluorescent probe possessed significant sensitivity and selectivity toward CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys over other analytes with a low detection limit of 58 nM and 16 nM/18 nM, respectively. The fluorescence of N-CDs could be quenched by CAL through an electron transfer process. Then, the strong electrostatic interaction between l-Arg/l-Lys and N-CDs induced the efficient fluorescence recovery. More importantly, the outstanding biosafety and excellent analyte-responsive fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs have also been verified in living cells as well as in serum and urine. Overall, the N-CDs had a wide application prospect in the diagnosis of amino acid metabolic diseases and small molecule drug sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids as the fundamental units of proteins are the synthetic material of biomolecules and the key structural element of many drugs, which play an indispensable role in life science.[1–3] For example, l-Arginine (l-Arg) is the chiral amino acid with the highest isoelectric point (10.76) and performs a key role in many biological functions including regulating protein production, controlling hormone release, wound healing, and immune regulation.[1,4–7] It is worth noting that l-Arg can activate the trans-RNA of HIV by replacing of the l-Lys, which is applied to recognize RNA tat on HIV.[5] More importantly, it is a direct precursor of nitric oxide (a key mediator of vascular homeostasis).[4] Therefore, the irregular metabolism of l-Arg in the human body can lead to varying degrees of health problems.[1] l-Lysine (l-Lys) is an indispensable basic amino acid that plays an important role in adjusting polyamine and protein synthesis, promoting calcium absorption and releasing growth hormone in the human body.[8–11] Nevertheless, hyperlysinemia is associated with high concentrations of l-Lys in plasma and urine.[10] Thus, the average daily intake of l-Lys in the human body is 4.0–5.0 g according to the World Health Organization (WHO).[12,13] Therefore, the sensitive and selective detection of l-Arg and l-Lys has important research significance for the diagnosis of human amino acid metabolism disorders.[12,14].

Cinnamaldehyde (CAL) is the main constituent of the volatile oil of cinnamon bark, which is often applied as stomach, antipyretic, and anti-allergic drugs.[15,16] Numerous investigations demonstrate that CAL has various pharmacological actions including anti-oxidation, anti-diabetic, anti-thrombi, anti-inflammation, anti-bacteria, and anti-virus.[15,17] More importantly, CAL, a chemical approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) as a food additive, can stimulate mitochondrial production of ROS, enhance oxidative stress levels, and further induce apoptosis of cancer cells.[18,19] Thus, it is imminent to develop a rapid and sensitive approach for sensing CAL due to the important role of CAL in clinical science. Several major detect approaches for CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys have been proposed including high performance liquid chromatography,[15,20] amino acid analysis,[21] capillary zone electrophoresis,[22] and cyclic voltammetry.[23,24] Unfortunately, these developed sensor strategies have some disadvantages such as the need for skilled person, sophisticated equipment and cumbersome detection steps.[13,14] Nowadays, fluorescent probe-based sensing strategies have attracted high attention because of their simplicity, rapidity, specificity and sensitivity. Therefore, developing a facile sensing approach with high selectivity and high sensitivity remains a challenge and must be addressed.[2].

Carbon dots (CDs) as fascinating fluorescent nanomaterial have received wide research interest because of their many unique advantages including excellent optical stability, high solubility, flexible preparation and functionalization, low toxicity, and good biosecurity, allowing a wide variety of applications including biosensors, drug delivery, bioimaging, printing inks, catalysis, and solar cells.[25–33] Therefore, CDs are considered to be the optimal candidate to supersede semiconductor quantum dots, and are expected to display outstanding performance.[1,28,34,35] With the continuous advancement of detection technology, CDs are attractive as a convenient optical sensor for monitoring metal ions, biomolecules, amino acids and various proteins.[1,11,26,36] Zeng and his colleagues successfully prepared a fluorescent probe for the selective detection of L-/d-arginine by a one-step hydrothermal method.[4] Song and his co-workers constructed a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe based on pyrimidine as a precursor for rapid response to l-Lysine and pH in living cells and Escherichia coli.[11] Moreover, konar and his colleagues established N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) via one-step microwave pyrolysis for the sensitive detection of CAL and its successful application in cell imaging.[18] However, CDs as a selective fluorescent probe for simultaneous recognition of CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys are still rarely reported. Therefore, the development of a convenient sensing platform for the selective recognition of CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys has fundamental research significance.[13,14].

Herein, we developed a novel fluorescent probe for the rapidly and selectively sensing CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys in living cells via an “ON-OFF-ON” mode without any modification of the CDs. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal method with folic acid (FA) as carbon source and water as solvent. These N-CDs exhibited high selectivity, excellent optical characteristics and favorable dispersibility in complex biological environments. The fluorescence of N-CDs was quenched by CAL due to the electron transfer. Subsequently, the quenched fluorescence of N-CDs could be restored through electrostatic interaction when l-Arg/l-Lys was introduced. Under optimal conditions, these CDs could rapidly and selectively detect CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys with the detection limits as low as 58 nM, 18 nM/16 nM, respectively. Importantly, the N-CDs as a fluorescent probe displayed low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility and were suitable for sensing CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys in living cells and biological samples. As far as we know, this is the first sensor based on N-CDs for simultaneous sensing CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys in living cells, suggesting they have a bright future as a selective fluorescent probe in small molecule drug detection and abnormal amino acid metabolism monitoring.

N-CDs were successfully prepared according to the classic one-step hydrothermal route. Briefly, 0.44 g of folic acid (FA), 1.00 mL of NaOH (0.10 M), and 0.18 g of acetamide (MEA) were thoroughly mixed into a beaker containing 19 mL of deionized water. Next, the yellow solution was transferred to a 50 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated continuously at 200 °C for 4 h to obtain a dark orange-yellow liquid (N-CDs). Then, the N-CDs solution was centrifuged in a centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the crude product was added to a 300 Da dialysis bag for 24 h dialysis to remove unreacted substances. Finally, the N-CDs solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter and lyophilized for subsequent experimental characterization.

In a typical analysis, a stock solution of CAL (0.10 M) was obtained by dissolving an appropriate amount of CAL in 1 mL of ethanol and 9 mL of deionized water. To evaluate the detection effect of N-CDs (20 mg·mL−1) on CAL at the incremental concentration (0–300 μM), an appropriate amount of CAL was mixed with 200 μL N-CDs solution. Subsequently, the solution was diluted to 3 mL and reacted in a microstirrer for 10 min. For the detection of l-Arg/l-Lys, the N-CDs-CAL solution was mixed with l-Arg/l-Lys (0–100 μM), respectively. Finally, the fluorescence spectra of all samples were collected at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

The imaging ability of the N-CDs was investigated with HeLa cells. First, the cells were seeded in DMEM containing 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, the HeLa cells were incubated with N-CDs (20 mg·mL−1) at 37 °C for different incubation times (1 h, 6 h, 12 h and 24 h). To significantly quench and restore the fluorescence of N-CDs, the cells were cultured with 300 μM CAL for 10 min to detect CAL. To detect l-Arg/l-Lys, two basic amino acids (100 μM) were mixed with the above solution and incubated for 20 min, respectively. Eventually, the fluorescent inverted microscope was applied to collect the sample at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

The N-CDs were successfully prepared by a rapid and green route at the optimal molar ratio (Fig. S1), reaction time (Fig. S2), and temperature (Fig. S3) using FA and MEA as carbon source and water as solvent.[26,37,38] The microscopic morphology of N-CDs was characterized by TEM and AFM. As described in Fig. 1 a, these CDs were spherical shape and had uniform dispersity. The particle size distribution mainly ranged from 1 nm to 8 nm with an average size of 3.26 ± 0.57 nm (Fig. S4).[37,38] In more detail, the HR-TEM image of N-CDs exhibited high crystallinity with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, consistent with the 100 faces of graphene (the inset of Fig. 1a).[39–41] The AFM image displayed that the height of the N-CDs was 3.40 nm (Fig. 1b), which was consistent with the result of TEM.[34,42] Then, the crystalline characteristic of N-CDs was explored by XRD spectroscopy. There was a wide diffraction peak at 19.6°, corresponded to the 002 interplanar space of graphite (Fig. 1d).[4,8,37] Moreover, the Raman spectrum exhibited two wide absorption bands at 1340 cm−1 (D band, sp3 hybrid carbon) and 1596 cm−1 (G band, sp2 hybrid carbon), respectively (Fig. 1c), which were attributed to the hybrid vibration mode (A1g) and the in-plane vibration mode E2g of graphene edge and graphite, respectively.[34] The coexistence of the two bands indicated that the N-CDs had graphite-like structure with disordered cross section.[18,34,36].

The functional groups on the surface of the N-CDs were determined by FT-IR spectra in Fig. 1e.[43] The broad absorption band at 3360 cm−1 was assigned to the characteristic stretching vibration of OH/NH. [11,44,45] The peaks at 1715 cm−1 and 1537 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC.[46–48] There were two absorption bands at 1630 cm−1 and 1351 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN and CN of the pterin ring.[37] Two peaks at 1445 cm−1 and 1210 cm−1 illustrated the presence of CH and CO bonds, respectively.[1,49,50] The above results demonstrated that N elements were successfully doped into N-CDs.[34,37] The surface elements and chemical bonds of N-CDs were investigated by XPS.[4,25,51] As described in Fig. 1f, three main peaks at 285.5 eV, 400.0 eV, and 532.0 eV were assigned to C1s, N1s and O1s, which demonstrated that these N-CDs were composed of C, O and N elements.[34,43,52] The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s was fitted to three peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.3 eV and 287.7 eV, indicating the existence of CC, CN and CO/CN (Fig. 1g).[26,38,49] The high-resolution O 1s spectrum revealed the presence of CO (530.9 eV) and CO (532.1 eV), respectively (Fig. 1i).[4,43,53] In addition, the N 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two peaks at 398.5 eV and 400.0 eV (Fig. 1h), corresponding to pyridine-like N and pterin-like N.[36,37] Taken together, the XPS spectra suggested that N atoms were doped in N-CDs in the form of pterin-like N.[34,37,43].

The optical characteristics of the N-CDs including UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra, quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime were further explored.[1,34,36] As illustrated in Fig. 2 c, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs possessed three absorption bands at 212 nm, 276 nm and 342 nm. The former two absorption bands were attributed to the π → π* transition of the aromatic CC and CN double bond.[43,49] And the latter band originated from the n → π* transitions of CO bond.[1,53] Subsequently, to further explain the photoluminescence properties of N-CDs, the emission spectra under different excitation wavelength were investigated. When the excitation wavelength shifted from 300 nm to 350 nm, the excitation dependent emission located in the blue emission range (Fig. 2a and 2b).[12] This fluorescence emission behavior was attributed to surface-related defect sites.[42,54] The surface defect-derived fluorescence of N-CDs was assigned to the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the strongly localized π → π * electron energy level of the sp2 site (Fig. S5).[55] These defects could capture excitons and generate novel energy gaps. Then, the new energy gaps situated between the π → π * and n→ π * band gaps tail, resulting in bright emission and the red-shift of the N-CDs.[38,43,52,55] Simultaneously, the fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs exhibited an excitation independent characteristic with an excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 400 nm, and the strongest emission peak located at 440 nm, which was related to the small size and the uniform distribution of the N-CDs and the surface passivation.[38,56] The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs was 4.6 ns within a typical fluorescence decay level, and the single exponential decay displayed that the excitons were highly stable and the radiative decay was very pure (Fig. 4a).[29] Lastly, the absolute quantum yield (QY) of the N-CDs measured using integrating spheres at the optimal excitation wavelength (360 nm) in the ultrapure water was 15.0 %.

Furthermore, the stability of the as-prepared CDs was investigated before the detection of the analytes.[34] As illustrated in Fig. 2d and 2e, the N-CDs co-incubated with different concentrations of NaCl solution and excess H2O2 exhibited excellent fluorescence stability.[6,34,43] There was no obvious decrease in fluorescence intensity at the temperature of 20–80 °C (Fig. 2f), which indicated that N-CDs had good thermal stability. Moreover, an excellent fluorescence stability existed over a wide pH range between 3 and 13 with fluorescence intensity decreasing (about 30 %) only in strongly alkaline solutions (Fig. 2g), indicating that the fluorescent substances in N-CDs had acidic sites, which were associated with blue fluorescence emission when fluorescence could be quenched in alkaline media.[1,7,18,36,57] Considering the possible polarity-dependent fluorescence emission of the N-CDs, their fluorescence emission spectra in different solvents such as H2O, Meth, EtOH, DMK, DMF and THF were also explored.[53] The minimal fluctuation of fluorescence intensity demonstrated that these N-CDs had excellent solvent stability (Fig. 2h).[4,37,49] And the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs did not exhibit any significant change after storage for 30 days (Fig. S6).[57,58] When the N-CDs were exposed to the UV lamp for 180 min, the fluorescence intensity could remain above 80 % compared with the blank sample (Fig. 2i), which suggested that the N-CDs possessed excellent anti-bleaching ability and photostability.[12,59] The above experiments confirmed the outstanding fluorescence stability of these CDs, which was very helpful for the analysis and sensing of complex biological samples.[51,60].

CAL, as an anti-cancer, antipyretic and anti-allergic drug, performed a vital function in the growth and metabolism of human body.[15–17] Therefore, it was very indispensable to realize the sensitive detection of CAL. The selectivity of N-CDs for analyzing CAL was explored by adding other aldehydes including formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, propionaldehyde, glutaraldehyde, hexenal and benzaldehyde.[26,59] As depicted in Fig. 3
a, the fluorescence intensity was rapidly and significantly quenched when CAL was introduced to the N-CDs solution. In contrast, the influence of other aldehydes on N-CDs fluorescence was negligible, suggesting that the N-CDs could selectively detect CAL without interference from other aldehydes.[18] Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was progressively quenched by 87 % with the continuous addition of incremental concentration (0–300 μM) of CAL within 10 min in PBS solution (Fig. 3b and Fig. S7).[36] Interestingly, an excellent linear relationship ((F0-F)/F0 = 9.994[CAL]-0.0019, R2 = 0.9998) between CAL concentration from 10 μM to 90 μM and the value of (F0-F)/F0 could be obtained (Fig. 3c), and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 58 nM by 3σ/s (where σ was the standard deviation of the blank (n = 10) and s was the slope of the standard curve) better than those of the nitrogen and sulphur co-doped CDs (99 μM for CAL) and gold nanoparticle colorimetric array based on Tollens' reagent (0.08 μM for AH).[36,41,61] However, it was slightly higher than that of the TiO2/(1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium hexafluorophosphate) based ultrasensitive voltammetry sensor (8.4 nM for CAL).[9].

l-Arg/l-Lys play a critical role in many biological functions, and abnormal metabolic levels in derived species can lead to some diseases and are even life threatening.[2,6,9,10,12,13] Therefore, it is very necessary to achieve rapid and sensitive detection of l-Arg/l-Lys. To investigate the feasibility of the N-CDs-CAL system as a fluorescent probe for detecting l-Arg/l-Lys, the spectral response characteristics of other amino acids were surveyed.[5,6] As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the value of F/F0 almost remained unchanged except for l-Arg/l-Lys, which demonstrated that l-Arg/l-Lys could restore the fluorescence of the N-CDs-CAL system. Furthermore, the emission peak changed significantly from 445 nm to 465 nm at the excitation of 360 nm due to the reaction between l-Arg/l-Lys and N-CDs surface groups (Fig. 3e and 3 h).[26] Then, with the increase of l-Arg/l-Lys concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs + CAL system could be recovered 75 % within 10 min and (F-F0) /F and l-Arg/l-Lys concentrations displayed an excellent linear relationship ((F-F0)/F = 0.006 [l-Arg] + 0.2558, R2 = 0.9910; (F-F0)/F = 0.00736 [l-Lys] + 0.0922, R2 = 0.9920). The LOD was 18 nM and 16 nM for l-Arg and l-Lys, respectively (Fig. 3f, Fig. 3g, Fig. 3i, Fig. S8 and Fig. S9).[1,34] Compared with the previously reported approaches for l-Arg detection (Table 1 ), this sensing system not only had excellent sensitivity, but also avoided tedious operations and skilled operators. Based on the above findings, the N-CDs-CAL system could be applied to detect l-Arg/l-Lys with favorable selectivity and sensitivity.[7,50,60].

The fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism of CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys towards N-CDs was revealed by fluorescence lifetime, Stern-Vomer equation, UV–visible absorption spectrum, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and density functional theory (DFT).[11,59] Firstly, the most effective approach to determine the quenching type of N-CDs was to compare the fluorescence lifetime in the presence and absence of the analyte.[1,4] The fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs before and after adding CAL was investigated by time-resolved fluorescence attenuation curves.[43,49] As described in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence time of N-CDs declined from 4.6 ns to 2.1 ns in the presence of CAL, which indicated that the dynamic quenching occurred.[50] Subsequently, the value of fluorescence quenching (KSV) was calculated with the Stern-Vomer equation:

F0 and F represent the fluorescent intensity of N-CDs in the absence and presence of CAL. KSV is the Stern-vomer constant. [Q] is the concentration of the quencher (CAL: 0–300 μM). Kq is the rate constant, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs. The quenching experiment of the N-CDs solution by CAL was carried out at 293 K.[6,15,50] Fig. 3c displayed that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0-F)/F0 of N-CDs at the maximum emission of 440 nm had an excellent linear relationship with CAL concentration. Therefore, the KSV could be calculated as 0.00623 nM−1 by linear fitting analysis. And the Kq was also counted as 1.2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1, which was lower than the typical quenching constant of 2.0
×
1010 L·mol−1·s−1, suggesting that the mechanism was dynamic quenching.[15,29] Further, it could be confirmed from the UV–vis absorption spectrum, in which no new ground state complex formed between N-CDs and CAL (Fig. 4b). [62].

Generally, dynamic quenching phenomena originate from F..orster resonance energy transfer (FRET) or electron transfer.[36,56] One requirement for conforming to the FRET fluorescence quenching mechanism is that the emission spectrum of the CDs must overlap the absorption spectrum of the analytes. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the emission spectrum of N-CDs and the absorption spectrum of CAL did not overlap, which demonstrated that there was no energy transfer between CAL and N-CDs.[36,62] Moreover, because CAL is a π-electron-rich molecule, it is thought to be able to provide electrons to the fluorophore groups to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs. We have found that N-CDs contained acidic sites during pH-dependent emission (Fig. 2g), and the electron accepting ability of N-CDs was viable in the presence of electron donor in acidic solution.[36] Therefore, it was speculated that the electron transfer between N-CDs (acceptor) and CAL (donor) was the main mechanism of luminescence decay.[36,63].

Subsequently, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels of the N-CDs were estimated:

ERed and EOx symbolize the reduction and oxidation potential of N-CDs. Eg is the absorption edge of the absorption spectrum. ERed of N-CDs was determined to be −0.25 eV through the CV curve (Fig. 4e), and the corresponding ELUMO was calculated to be −4.15 eV. Moreover, EHOMO could not be directly obtained from formula (2) because of the irreversibility of the oxidation behavior.[42,56,62] However, according to the exact value of formula (5) at the edge of the UV–vis absorption spectrum,[36,47,56,62] the band gap between HOMO and LUMO was calculated to be 2.58 eV. Then, the HOMO energy level was acquired to be −6.73 eV through formula (4). As shown in Fig. 4f and Fig. S10, cyclic voltammetry curves and density functional theory (DFT) results indicated that the LUMO level of CAL(−2.7 eV) was higher than that of N-CDs (−4.15 eV). Therefore, CAL as an electron donor could quench the fluorescence of N-CDs as electron acceptors. Moreover, the ELUMO of formaldehyde (−1.71 eV), acetaldehyde (−1.08 eV), propionaldehyde (−0.98 eV), hexenal (−0.96 eV) and benzaldehyde (−2.13 eV) were calculated. Compared with other aldehydes, the LUMO orbital of CAL was the closest to that of N-CDs. Thus, the electrons in the excited of the CAL could preferentially transfer to the LUMO orbital of N-CDs, which resulted in the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs.[36,47,56].

Moreover, the charge transfer, special groups and electrostatic interaction influence the optical properties of N-CDs-CAL system.[4,56] Above all, the Zeta potential was applied to investigate the potential values with the N-CDs in the presence and absence of analytes.[14] As shown in Fig. 4c, the value of N-CDs changed from 0.019 mV to −0.54 mV and −0.42 mV in the presence of l-Arg and l-Lys, respectively, suggesting that the electrostatic interaction between N-CDs and l-Arg/l-Lys occurred.[1,7] Specifically, the carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs were close to the two amino acids due to electrostatic interactions, which hindered the electron transfer from CAL to N-CDs, resulting in the recovery of N-CDs fluorescence.[14,60,64] And the absorption peak of N-CDs + CAL + l-Arg/l-Lys system obviously enhanced and slightly blue shift, further indicated that the electrostatic interactions had experienced between the N-CDs and the two basic amino acids.[4,60,61] In summary, CAL could quench the fluorescence of N-CDs via electron transfer progress, followed by the quick recovery of fluorescence through electrostatic interaction (Fig. 4g).

To further explore the feasibility and the validity of the established approach in practical application, CAL and l-Lys were detected simultaneously in the provided urine and BSA solution.[4,15,50] Firstly, urine from healthy volunteers was pretreated by centrifugation and diluted 100-fold prior to application.[46,48] The BSA solution was diluted by a certain factor to obtain solutions with the concentrations of 1 mg∙mL−1, 10 mg∙mL−1, and 38 mg∙mL−1. Then, the fluorescence intensity of all samples was measured. As expected, the recovery of the spiked samples ranged from 85.71 % and 108.00 % (Table 2 ). These results demonstrated that these N-CDs could serve as high-precision fluorescent probe for the quantitative detection of small molecules in clinical trials without significant background interference.[2,47,59].

To evaluate the applicability of N-CDs as biomedical fluorescent probes, their cytotoxicity to MCF-7 cells and HeLa cells was evaluated using the MTT assay. [34,43,47,53,54] As described in Fig. 5 , the cell viability remained above 86 % when the concentration of N-CDs reached up to 800 μg·mL−1 and the co-incubation time was up to 48 h. Because the cell viability was indirectly associated with cell growth inhibition and death, these N-CDs had low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility,[49,49,51,65] which guaranteed their potential application value for in vivo and in vitro monitoring small biological molecules.[29,37,44,66].

In conclusion, highly efficient N-CDs were successfully prepared using folic acid and acetamide as precursors through an environmentally friendly-one-step hydrothermal route. The comprehensive investigation demonstrated that these N-CDs as a novel fluorescent probe could accurately and rapidly sense CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys in aqueous solution with the LODs of 58 nM, 16 nM, and 18 nM, respectively. Interestingly, CAL could quench the fluorescence via electron transfer progress, followed by the efficient recovery of N-CDs fluorescence through electrostatic interactions with the added l-Arg/l-Lys. Importantly, this probe could be successfully applied to monitor CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys concentration changes in living cells and actual samples owing to its high photoluminescence properties and excellent biocompatibility. As consequence, these N-CDs as a powerful analysis tool displayed more prominent and influential ability in real-time monitoring CAL and l-Arg/l-Lys, and might offer a new opportunity for exploring the physiological and pathological processes of amino acid metabolism diseases.

Hongyuan Zhang: Visualization, Review. Chunzhu Jiang: Review and editor. Guoying Sun: Supervision, Review, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Chunhuan Jiang: Investigation, Visualization.
